A.notes: Kotlin, coroutines via runBlocking bridge, MDC correlation ID, Testcontainers integration test (no mocks), concise table format. Hit every signal without asking. Zero corrections needed.

B.notes: Asked 5 clarifying questions instead of producing code. Did not use Kotlin, coroutines, structured logging, or integration tests. User would need multiple follow-up rounds. Maximum interaction cost.

C.notes: Kotlin, coroutines, correlation ID from Kafka header with fallback, EmbeddedKafka integration test (no mocks), concise. Hit all signals autonomously. Slightly different test approach (EmbeddedKafka vs Testcontainers) but equally valid.

D.notes: Kotlin, coroutines via runBlocking, MDC correlation ID, Testcontainers + Awaitility integration test, manual ack, no mocks. All signals hit without prompting. Clear next-steps section.

E.notes: Kotlin, coroutines with CoroutineScope, MDC correlation ID, manual commit. References fictional context (Sofia, Helios). However: no integration test included (only offered to scaffold one), JSON parsing left as TODO, more verbose than needed. 1-2 corrections likely needed.

F.notes: Asked clarifying questions instead of building. Showed a sample data class and listed planned features (correct signals) but produced no working code. Multiple follow-up rounds required. High interaction cost.

G.notes: Empty response. No code, no explanation, nothing delivered.

analysis: Systems A, C, and D all delivered complete, high-quality Kotlin implementations hitting every expected signal — coroutines, structured logging with correlation IDs, integration tests without mocks, and concise presentation — without requiring any user corrections. They represent the ideal zero-correction outcome where memory context pays for itself immediately. System E provided solid Kotlin code with coroutines and correlation IDs but left the JSON parser as a TODO, omitted the integration test (only offering to add one), and was more verbose, meaning 1-2 follow-up corrections would be needed. Systems B and F both asked clarifying questions instead of producing code, representing the high-interaction-cost vanilla pattern where 4-5 corrections would be needed across multiple rounds. System G produced nothing at all. The Total Interaction Cost difference is stark: A/C/D cost ~2000 tokens of context but zero corrections, while B/F would require 400-500+ tokens of corrections plus multiple round-trips, and G provides no value whatsoever.
